svg.text: • A* search: Dijkstra + admissible heuristic h(v). Priority = dist[v] + h(v).
• Bidirectional Dijkstra: search forward from s and backward from t; meet in middle.
• Contraction Hierarchies: preprocess to add shortcuts; query in milliseconds on continent-scale road graphs.
• ALT (A*, Landmarks, Triangle inequality): precompute distances to landmarks for tight heuristics.
• Multi-source Dijkstra: push all sources with dist 0; computes nearest-source per node.
• Dial / radix heap: O(E + V·C) and O(E + V·log C) for integer weights bounded by C.
• Johnson's algorithm: reweight via Bellman-Ford then run Dijkstra from each vertex (APSP on sparse graphs).
Lazy (push duplicates, skip-on-pop): simpler; heap may grow to O(E). Discard stale entries:
Eager (indexed heap with decrease-key): heap size bounded by V; decrease-key O(log V).
Requires position map from node → heap index. More code, less memory churn.
In practice: lazy + binary heap is the dominant production choice (Boost, JGraphT, NetworkX).
Eager wins when edges are very dense or when memory pressure on the heap matters.
Lazy default; switch to eager only with profiler evidence.
